title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Space Tourism on Science

texts.A: [37]The burgeoning industry of space tourism is regularly sold to the public as a gateway to the stars for all of humanity, but it is currently nothing more than an extraordinarily expensive vanity project designed exclusively for the ultra-wealthy.[/37] [38]The environmental cost of a single rocket launch, measured in terms of carbon dioxide emissions and long-lasting atmospheric soot deposited directly in the upper stratosphere, is truly astronomical when compared to the trivial benefit of a few brief minutes of weightlessness for a handful of billionaires.[/38] We are exhausting finite engineering talent and vast capital on spectacles for the elite. [40]We should be focusing our precious aerospace engineering talent on urgently needed climate-monitoring satellites and planetary defense systems, not on constructing luxury 'space hotels' that offer zero scientific value and serve only to widen the already vast gulf between the global elite and the rest of humanity.[/40] This is a profound misallocation of resources.
texts.B: To dismissively characterize space tourism as a mere vanity project is to fundamentally misunderstand how transformative technological innovation actually works in practice throughout history. Every major breakthrough in aviation, from the earliest tentative commercial passenger flights to the revolutionary development of the jet engine and supersonic travel, was initially bankrolled by the wealthy few before becoming accessible to the many through economies of scale and mass production. The high ticket prices of today effectively represent the research and development budget that will make genuinely affordable space access a practical reality for future generations of ordinary citizens. [39]Furthermore, the vigorous and competitive involvement of the private sector has already and dramatically reduced the cost per kilogram of launching scientific payloads into orbit, which constitutes an enormously direct and tangible benefit to the global research and scientific community.[/39] [37]Space tourism is ultimately the economic engine that will finally render the long-term colonization and settlement of the solar system genuinely and practically viable.[/37] Progress always requires pioneers.
texts.C: [38]I share the widespread and legitimate concern about the immediate and measurable environmental impact of chemical rocket fuels on the upper stratosphere, but the rational and genuinely proportionate solution is clearly the accelerated development of green propulsion technologies, not a sweeping blanket ban on the entire emerging space tourism industry.[/38] [40]However, I fully agree that the current commercial fixation on brief sub-orbital hops offering nothing more than a few precious minutes of weightlessness is scientifically hollow and ethically difficult to defend.[/40] A more responsible and forward-looking framework is urgently needed from regulators. We should establish requirements ensuring that every commercial flight carries a suite of autonomous scientific instruments to study the upper atmosphere and measure pollution levels in real time. If we systematically treat every tourist craft as a mobile research laboratory, we can genuinely transform a luxury indulgence into a valuable large-scale data-gathering exercise for environmental science. The Overview Effect could also be a catalyst for global environmental awareness.
texts.D: The real and most underappreciated danger of the accelerating space tourism boom is not primarily environmental but critically orbital. It is the so-called Kessler Syndrome—the theoretical but scientifically plausible scenario in which a single high-velocity collision between satellites or debris fragments triggers a catastrophic cascading chain reaction, generating a persistent and largely untrackable cloud of high-speed debris that renders the entire low-Earth orbit band completely unusable for multiple generations. Casually adding thousands of commercial tourist craft and their associated launch vehicles to an already dangerously crowded orbital environment is, by any reasonable engineering assessment, a recipe for irreversible catastrophe of the highest order. [39]While I broadly share the view that private investment represents a necessary and genuinely welcome component of long-term space progress and development, I am firmly and unequivocally convinced that all commercial space operations must be subject to strict, binding, and internationally enforceable regulation administered by a credible international governing body with genuine authority and enforcement powers.[/39] We cannot permit a Wild West atmosphere in space where corporations recklessly jeopardize vital infrastructure.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the long-term justification for space tourism?
questions.explanation: Expert B justifies it as the 'R&D' for future travel and a way to make colonization viable. Expert A argues it is a 'vanity project' with 'no scientific value' and an unjustifiable environmental cost.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the environmental or atmospheric consequences of rocket launches?
questions.explanation: Expert A mentions the 'environmental cost' and 'carbon emissions.' Expert C explicitly shares the 'concern about the immediate environmental impact of chemical rockets.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the role of private companies in advancing space technology?
questions.explanation: Expert B says private involvement has 'drastically reduced' costs. Expert D also 'shares the view that private investment is necessary for progress,' though they emphasize the need for regulation.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether commercial space flights can be considered scientifically useful in their current form?
questions.explanation: Expert C believes they are 'scientifically hollow' in their current sub-orbital form (unless changes are made). Expert A agrees, stating they offer 'no scientific value' at all.
